title: Treat the initial PRD as a rough intent draft
context: The first document is a brain dump for discussion, not a locked product direction.
decision: Use the PRD only as initial intent. Start by locking problem statement, scope, source eligibility, architecture, and execution model before implementation.
consequences: Avoid overfitting architecture to unvalidated draft details.
Expect docs and implementation plans to evolve as research lands.

title: Scope v1 around narrative screen media and exclude music
context: The motivating discovery problem is cross-domain recommendations and relationships for shows, movies, and anime. Music aggregation is a separate hard problem with mature external efforts.
decision: Make anime, TV, and movies the initial first-class domains. Exclude music from the initial scope.
consequences: Anime is not modeled as a TV subtype.
Music sources and schemas are deferred.
Book/source-material links may be used as relationship evidence without making books a full v1 domain.

title: Make anime first class
context: Anime has unique relationship, title, season/cour, OVA/ONA/special, and source-material realities. The manami project already proves substantial offline anime cross-reference work.
decision: Treat anime as a first-class domain alongside TV and movies, and leverage manami-project/anime-offline-database as a key source and design precedent.
consequences: Anime-specific schemas and relationship handling are expected.
Manami is a backbone candidate, not a thin afterthought wrapper.
Anime relationship richness can inform the shared relationship graph model.

title: Classify providers by admissible use
context: Provider research showed many popular APIs do not permit durable offline redistribution even for non-commercial open projects.
decision: Every source must be classified by role: BACKBONE_SOURCE, ID_SOURCE, LOCAL_EVIDENCE, RUNTIME_ONLY, or BLOCKED. Credentials unlock access but do not imply redistribution rights.
consequences: Provider adapters must enforce publishability at write time.
Restricted sources may help local matching but cannot leak raw payloads or copied metadata into public artifacts.
Source eligibility is central architecture, not a README footnote.

title: Use containerized execution as the local boundary
context: Long-running metadata jobs are risky if they only work on one developer machine. The project should match CI and scheduled execution from the start.
decision: Do not run project pipeline tasks directly on host Python. Local development uses Docker/Compose and Woodpecker CLI against the same command surface used by CI.
consequences: The Docker image is the canonical runtime.
Host Python packages are not validation dependencies.
Local bootstrap can use mounted caches and personal tokens, but it still runs inside containers.

title: Use Python with strict tooling inside the container
context: Python is not the preferred personal language, but it has the strongest data and Hugging Face ecosystem for this work.
decision: Use Python 3.12+, uv, Polars, PyArrow, DuckDB, Pydantic v2, httpx, Typer, huggingface_hub, sentence-transformers, pytest, Ruff, and Pyright inside the project container.
consequences: Keep Python code typed, linted, and production-shaped rather than notebook-shaped.
Do not rely on globally installed Python tools.
Use Pyright and Ruff as CI gates from the beginning.

title: Use polite cached batch LLMs for judgment, not generation
context: Free/open LLM capacity is available for open-source/non-commercial projects, but the project must not spam providers or generate unsupported facts.
decision: Use OpenAI-compatible LLM providers, initially OpenRouter, for bounded judgment tasks such as ambiguous match review, tag normalization, and relationship classification. Cache by input hash, prompt version, task, and model; publish decision logs.
consequences: LLM calls happen after deterministic and embedding filters.
Quota exhaustion should pause work and leave queued tasks rather than fail the whole pipeline.
LLM outputs are auditable derived judgments, not source metadata.

title: Prefer free-access reproducibility for providers and model inference
context: The project is non-commercial and open, but paid access to providers with ambiguous terms weakens the legal and community posture by making outputs look like privileged derivative works. Free/open access does not grant redistribution rights by itself, but it makes the pipeline easier for ordinary contributors to reproduce and supports the argument that public artifacts are built from public inputs plus auditable transformations.
decision: Canonical published pipelines should prefer open bulk downloads, public free tiers, and free/open inference providers. Paid or contract access may be used only for private experimentation or explicitly non-published local evidence unless a later accepted decision documents redistribution rights and approves publication use.
consequences: Add PAID_EXPERIMENT_ONLY as a source role for privileged access that must not shape public artifacts.
Free-tier budgets and caches are part of policy enforcement, not only cost control.
Embedding and LLM providers should default to free/open-access routes when feasible.
Credentials unlock access but never prove publishability.

title: Select free-access default models for embeddings and LLM judgment
context: Canonical runs need cloud-accessible model inference that can run from GitHub Actions or Woodpecker without paid/private infrastructure. Current free offerings are strongest for embeddings on Cloudflare Workers AI and strongest for structured judgment through specific OpenRouter free models. Gemini embeddings are free and strong, but Google documents free-tier content use for product improvement, so they should not be the default canonical path.
decision: Use Cloudflare Workers AI `@cf/baai/bge-m3` as the canonical embedding model. Use OpenRouter `qwen/qwen3-next-80b-a3b-instruct:free` as the primary structured-judgment model, with `nvidia/nemotron-3-super-120b-a12b:free` and `inclusionai/ling-2.6-flash:free` as fallbacks. Keep Gemini `gemini-embedding-001`, Gemini `gemini-embedding-2`, Jina embeddings, Hugging Face feature extraction, and OpenRouter free embedding models as benchmark/fallback routes only.
consequences: Model identity must be persisted in every embedding and LLM decision artifact.
`openrouter/free` is not acceptable for canonical decisions because dynamic routing weakens reproducibility.
Embedding budget enforcement should track Cloudflare neurons and input tokens.
Model choices must be re-reviewed before the first public dataset release.

title: Add keyless CI precursor before provider credentials
context: Before API keys exist, CI should still prove the container runtime, locked dependency install, CLI, code quality gates, policy docs, offline data stack, mocked model contracts, and recoverable artifact flow. This avoids waiting on credentials to discover basic pipeline or CI issues.
decision: GitHub Actions and Woodpecker should run keyless precursor checks: Docker build, CLI smoke, Ruff, Pyright, pytest policy/data/model tests, and a tiny Parquet artifact generation step. GitHub Actions should upload that smoke artifact for recovery-path rehearsal.
consequences: The repo can validate meaningful architecture behavior without secrets.
Offline Parquet/DuckDB and mocked model budget/cache behavior become CI gates.
Provider credentials can be added later without conflating auth setup with runtime correctness.

title: Use empirical OpenRouter benchmark results for free LLM defaults
context: The docs/catalog-selected primary model `qwen/qwen3-next-80b-a3b-instruct:free` was upstream rate-limited during the first real Manami-derived benchmark. The benchmark tested 30 current free OpenRouter text-output routes on a Cowboy Bebop identity/relationship judgment from manami-project/anime-offline-database release 2026-14.
decision: Use `inclusionai/ling-2.6-flash:free` as the current primary LLM judgment model. Use `inclusionai/ling-2.6-1t:free`, `liquid/lfm-2.5-1.2b-instruct:free`, `openai/gpt-oss-20b:free`, and `baidu/qianfan-ocr-fast:free` as fallbacks. Do not use `openrouter/free` for canonical decisions, and do not restore `qwen/qwen3-next-80b-a3b-instruct:free` as primary without a later benchmark showing reliable availability.
consequences: Model selection is now evidence-led rather than docs-led.
Provider upstream 429s are expected benchmark data, not only transient failures.
Future model swaps need benchmark artifacts or a new accepted decision.

title: Throttle live free-model benchmarks by default
context: The first OpenRouter free-model benchmark hit every discovered free text model and consumed too much shared free-provider capacity. Free-access reproducibility requires being polite to provider free tiers, not merely avoiding paid services.
decision: Benchmark tooling must default to a small model limit, wait between requests, and require an explicit `--all` flag for full-catalog runs. Full OpenRouter free-model benchmarks should be rare, intentional, and documented.
consequences: The benchmark script now defaults to five models and a four-second delay.
Future agents should not run all-model benchmarks casually.
Provider politeness is treated as part of the project trust boundary.

title: Add Z.ai as a serialized free LLM candidate
context: Z.ai offers GLM Flash models that are free for users with an account and API token, with concurrency limits rather than per-request daily caps. The official docs expose an OpenAI-compatible chat completions endpoint and JSON object response mode. The project now has split Z.ai API key fields available locally.
decision: Add Z.ai configuration for `glm-4.7-flash` and `glm-4.5-flash`, support either split key id/secret fields or an already-joined `id.secret` API key, send the resulting API key directly as `Authorization: Bearer <ZAI_API_KEY>`, and enforce a default max concurrency of one for this lane. Treat Z.ai as a preferred benchmark candidate before promoting it to canonical LLM judgment primary.
consequences: Z.ai live calls must be serialized unless account limits change.
The Manami benchmark should be run against Z.ai before canonical model promotion.
The repo can support Z.ai whether the local secret field contains only the secret half or the joined API key.

title: Keep OpenRouter primary after direct Z.ai GLM rerank
context: A direct Z.ai GLM benchmark was run only against `glm-4.7-flash` and `glm-4.5-flash`, then merged with cached OpenRouter benchmark artifacts. `glm-4.5-flash` produced valid JSON and the correct Cowboy Bebop identity/relationship judgment at 6569 ms. `glm-4.7-flash` returned Z.ai overload code `1305`. Cached OpenRouter rows still contained faster correct models, including `inclusionai/ling-2.6-flash:free` at 1417 ms.
decision: Keep `inclusionai/ling-2.6-flash:free` as the current canonical structured-judgment primary. Keep Z.ai as a serialized direct-provider fallback/benchmark lane, with `glm-4.5-flash` preferred over `glm-4.7-flash` until a later run shows `glm-4.7-flash` is reliably reachable.
consequences: The rerank uses cached OpenRouter artifacts and fresh Z.ai-only rows instead of rerunning OpenRouter.
Z.ai is attractive because it avoids daily request caps, but availability and latency still matter for canonical selection.
Future benchmark tooling should make provider-level cache reuse explicit.

title: Promote model benchmarks to a scheduled safety subsystem
context: Model/provider selection will span many free and free-tier providers, recurring cron runs, LLM judgment fixtures, embedding fixtures, and future enhanced corpora. One-off scripts made it too easy to copy incorrect auth assumptions across providers, as seen in the Z.ai JWT mistake.
decision: Create a benchmark architecture with versioned fixtures, provider contracts, explicit auth schemes, per-provider budgets, cache-first execution, and cron-safe schedules. Provider adapters must follow checked-in contracts and include redacted request-shape tests before live benchmark use.
consequences: No new provider should be benchmarked without an official-docs-backed contract.
Cron runs should refresh small slices by default and reuse cached results.
Gemini free-tier LLM and embedding models enter as benchmark candidates with the documented free-tier data-use caveat.

title: Keep Gemini free tier as benchmark-only after first direct run
context: The first direct Gemini REST benchmark used `x-goog-api-key` against `generateContent` and tested seven free-of-charge text candidates from the provider contract. `gemini-2.5-flash-lite` produced a correct valid JSON judgment at 1049 ms. `gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview` also produced a correct valid JSON judgment at 6364 ms. `gemini-3-flash-preview` and `gemini-2.5-flash` were reachable but returned prose under the initial structured-output payload. `gemini-2.5-pro` returned free-tier quota 0 for this key/account, while `gemma-4` and `gemini-2.5-flash-lite-preview-09-2025` were not found for v1beta generateContent.
decision: Treat `gemini-2.5-flash-lite` as the strongest Gemini free-tier LLM judgment benchmark candidate for now, but keep Gemini free-tier output out of canonical model selection until a policy decision accepts the documented free-tier data-use terms.
consequences: Gemini is now high-value benchmark evidence, especially for comparing free non-OpenRouter routes.
The Gemini provider contract should be refined with listModels before rerunning unavailable model IDs.
Embedding benchmarks for `gemini-embedding-001` and `gemini-embedding-2` still need a dedicated embedding fixture and scoring lane.

title: Record Gemini embedding latency baseline
context: A first direct Gemini embedding latency run used `x-goog-api-key` and `embedContent` against the two free-of-charge embedding models in the Gemini provider contract. The input was one short Cowboy Bebop-related text. No raw vectors were written to artifacts, only dimensions and latency.
decision: Record `gemini-embedding-2` as the faster Gemini embedding benchmark candidate for this tiny baseline: 3072 dimensions in 323 ms versus `gemini-embedding-001` at 3072 dimensions in 485 ms. Keep both benchmark-only until embedding quality fixtures exist and the Gemini free-tier data-use caveat is resolved.
consequences: Gemini embeddings are operationally reachable and fast enough to include in future quality benchmarks.
Latency alone is not enough to choose an embedding model for canonical datasets.
Future embedding artifacts should continue avoiding raw-vector logs unless the output is explicitly meant to be a dataset artifact.

title: Fix Gemini 3 Flash benchmark payload
context: The first Gemini run showed `gemini-3-flash-preview` was reachable but returned prose instead of JSON under the initial payload. Official structured-output docs list Gemini 3 Flash Preview as supporting structured outputs. Debug variants showed the model could produce the correct JSON when the payload was simplified, schema descriptions/property ordering were added, and the output cap was raised from 220 to 500 tokens.
decision: Use the simplified Gemini structured-output payload for Gemini judgment benchmarks: no `systemInstruction`, JSON-only instruction in user content, schema descriptions and `propertyOrdering`, `responseMimeType=application/json`, `responseJsonSchema`, and `maxOutputTokens=500`. Record `gemini-3-flash-preview` as a valid benchmark candidate after it returned the correct Cowboy Bebop judgment at 2294 ms.
consequences: The earlier Gemini 3 Flash failure was a harness issue, not evidence against the model.
Gemini judgment benchmarks should be rerun with the fixed payload before comparing all Gemini candidates.
Prompt/payload variants should be treated as part of benchmark recipe versioning.

title: Use contract-qualified model allowlists for rankings
context: Benchmark results became hard to trust because free-tier model discovery mixed stable candidates, preview routes, task-misaligned specialists, unavailable IDs, and nondeterministic routers in the same ranking tables. The OpenRouter lane in particular was still discovering all free models instead of using a reviewed allowlist.
decision: Make provider contracts the source of truth for model qualification. Per-model entries must declare task, stability, API capability, and `qualified_for_ranking`. Ranking runs use only models explicitly qualified for the task; previews, routers, unstable models, and excluded specialists remain exploration-only.
consequences: OpenRouter ranking runs now use a checked-in allowlist rather than all free models.
Tests fail if provider models omit task or ranking qualification metadata.
Future reruns should be labeled by qualified recipe version so old exploratory results do not pollute current rankings.

title: Treat the fresh qualified rerun as the current LLM benchmark baseline
context: After tightening provider contracts, model allowlists, and the Gemini structured-output recipe, the benchmark suite was rerun against the currently qualified OpenRouter, Gemini, and Z.ai candidates only. The clean rerun produced seven correct valid-JSON results, led by `liquid/lfm-2.5-1.2b-instruct:free`, `gemini-2.5-flash-lite`, `openai/gpt-oss-20b:free`, `openai/gpt-oss-120b:free`, `gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview`, `glm-4.5-flash`, and `gemini-3-flash-preview`. Several other candidates were either upstream rate-limited or returned truncated/empty output.
decision: Use the fresh qualified rerun as the current benchmark baseline and treat earlier broader model sweeps as exploratory only. Do not lock a canonical LLM judgment default yet; expand the fixture corpus first and re-evaluate once the leaders have been tested on more than one case.
consequences: Repo docs should stop presenting earlier exploratory winners such as `ling` as current defaults.
Leaderboard language should distinguish benchmark leaders from canonical defaults.
Future model promotions need to beat the current qualified baseline on a broader fixture set, not just one fixture.

title: Document local LLM prompt-tuning runs as benchmark-only
context: The repo had local Ollama and OpenAI-compatible benchmark harnesses but no runbook for using them while tuning prompts. Local prompt work spans multiple commands and needs a reproducible command surface that respects the containerized execution boundary.
decision: Add `docs/runbooks/local-llm-prompt-tuning.md` as the runbook for local LLM prompt evaluation. Treat these runs as prompt and inference benchmarks, not model fine-tuning or source metadata generation.
consequences: Local LLM prompt experiments use Docker/Compose for project code and explicit local model-server endpoints.
Prompt variants should be versioned through output naming or code before results are compared.
Accepted prompt or model-selection changes still require decision-log entries before becoming canonical.

title: Use the best practical local LLM as the prompt measuring stick
context: Prior prompt wording for dataset connections was ambiguous. Larger or higher-token cloud models could infer the intended relationship judgments, but tightening the prompt and parameters let lower-quality models extract the relevant information just as well. The local Mac is not inference-optimized, so the useful target is the best model that runs reliably on this device, not the largest possible model.
decision: Use the strongest practical local LLM on this Mac as the prompt and parameter measuring stick. Tune relationship-judgment prompts against that local baseline first, then compare cloud variants against the frozen local recipe instead of relying on cloud model size to compensate for ambiguous prompts.
consequences: Prompt clarity is treated as a model-selection lever, not only a code-quality concern.
Cloud benchmark candidates should be evaluated against the local measuring-stick recipe and fixture set.
Do not promote a larger or higher-token cloud model merely because it handles a vague prompt better than local models.

title: Define the project problem as a reusable media relationship dataset
context: Current media discovery tools depend on fragmented sources, provider-specific APIs, just-in-time relationship reconstruction, and uneven recommendation quality. Public facts exist across many places, but identity links, relationship edges, provenance, and recommendation-ready context are not collected into a durable dataset that future tools can reuse.
decision: Define the project's problem statement around building an open, repeatable dataset compiler for narrative screen media. The dataset should collect admissible public information, preserve provenance, normalize identity and relationship graphs, support embeddings and RAG, and provide auditable LLM-assisted judgments where deterministic evidence is insufficient.
consequences: `docs/problem-statement.md` becomes the durable statement of why the project exists.
The project optimizes for reusable dataset artifacts rather than a hosted recommendation service.
Future corpus, schema, and benchmark work should trace back to better media search, recommendation, relationship discovery, and retrieval use cases.

title: Limit the project product surface to dataset artifacts
context: The project could be confused with an API, application, hosted search service, recommendation engine, graph browser, or RAG serving layer because downstream tools are expected to use the dataset for those purposes. That is not the intent. The repo should compile and publish data that others can inspect or build on, not provide the consumption product itself.
decision: The project product surface is limited to reusable dataset artifacts, manifests, provenance, documentation, and refresh/publish tooling. Do not build or scope a hosted API, direct application, user-facing consumption layer, personalized recommendation service, or RAG serving interface in this repo unless a later accepted decision changes the product boundary.
consequences: Consumption belongs to downstream implementers, not this project.
Query previews and smoke tools are allowed only as validation or artifact-inspection utilities, not as product surfaces.
Architecture decisions should optimize artifact quality, schema clarity, provenance, and reproducibility over serving concerns.

title: Use John as the first downstream-consumer persona
context: The project needs a concrete consumer context while preserving the boundary that this repo only publishes dataset artifacts. John is a developer building an external mobile or web application for someone who wants recommendations after watching a show such as `The Expanse`. Existing APIs and web search are too fragmented and nondeterministic for the application experience he wants to build.
decision: Document John as the first downstream-consumer persona in `docs/personas/john-downstream-app-developer.md`. Use this persona to focus schema, relationship, provenance, graph, embedding, and progressive-enhancement decisions around artifact surfaces that an external implementer can consume.
consequences: The dataset should expose weighted, explainable relationship and similarity surfaces rather than a single product-specific recommendation answer.
Consumption remains outside this repo; John's application owns ranking, UI, personalization, and query handling.
Future dataset-shape discussions should be able to explain how the artifact helps or does not help this persona.

title: Use Alex as the anime discovery and progressive-enhancement persona
context: Alex wants to find anime like `Golden Time` or anime where adult main characters are in university, with signals such as adult cast, university setting, contemporary world, emotional continuity, conclusion, and related themes like amnesia. Existing APIs, tag systems, and normalized cross-reference projects still leave this as manual detective work, and newly released similar shows need to become discoverable after dataset refreshes.
decision: Document Alex as a downstream-consumer persona in `docs/personas/alex-anime-discovery.md`. Use this persona to focus anime domain-profile design, faceted discovery, retrieval-ready text, relationship weighting, evidence, and progressive enhancement for new releases and newly discovered evidence.
consequences: Anime remains first-class because its taxonomy affects discovery quality, not because the whole project is anime-only.
Dataset surfaces should support concept queries such as adult university anime without owning the external search UI.
Scheduled refresh and enhancement work should connect new qualifying works into existing discovery patterns through versioned dataset snapshots.

title: Continuously review provider terms before shaping published artifacts
context: Most source providers offer public access or authorized token-based access, and the project has tokens for some providers so it can identify itself rather than behave anonymously. Those providers are doing valuable work. The project exists because the information is scattered and hard to use across modern query workflows, not because provider rights or terms can be ignored.
decision: Every existing and new provider must undergo explicit terms-of-service, license, attribution, redistribution, cache, and rate-limit review before its data shapes published artifacts. Credentials, public endpoints, and access tokens are access mechanisms only; they do not imply redistribution rights. Document the repeatable review process in `docs/runbooks/provider-review.md` and keep evidence in `docs/source-admissibility-and-rate-limits.md`.
consequences: Provider review is continuous implementation work, not a one-time research task.
Source roles and publishable fields must be justified with evidence links before canonical use.
The dataset should focus on respectful collation, normalization, provenance, and retrieval-ready structure across admissible sources.

title: Treat similarity candidates as a post-v1 derived surface
context: Recommendation-like behavior should come from dataset surfaces that downstream consumers can combine, not from this project producing one final recommendation list. Similarity candidates are useful, but they depend on stable entities, relationships, facets, retrieval text, embeddings, and evidence. They should not block v1 or force a full source rebuild when scoring logic changes.
decision: Make `similarity_candidates` a post-v1 progressive enhancement. Keep v1 focused on base artifact surfaces such as entities, titles, external IDs, relationships, evidence, facets, retrieval text, embeddings, LLM judgments, provenance, and manifests. When similarity logic changes, accept the cost of recomputing the affected recipe-versioned derived table from existing artifacts instead of re-ingesting sources or rebuilding unrelated base tables.
consequences: The project can ship useful v1 artifacts before precomputed similarity rankings exist.
Similarity scoring recipes must be versioned so downstream consumers can choose when to adopt new candidate tables.
Architecture should separate durable base surfaces from recomputable derived enhancement surfaces.

title: Keep inferred values in judgments before facet materialization
context: Discovery use cases need normalized facets such as adult cast, university setting, contemporary world, and narrative closure, but not every useful attribute is directly present as a source field or simple tag mapping. Mixing inferred claims directly into facets would make it hard for downstream consumers to distinguish source-backed normalized values from model-assisted, human-reviewed, or complex deterministic judgments.
decision: Store inferred values only in judgment surfaces first. Materialize inferred values into normalized facets only through explicit materialization rules with confidence thresholds, provenance links, and recipe versions. Keep source tags, normalized facets, and inferred judgments as separate artifact surfaces.
consequences: Facets remain queryable while preserving auditability for inferred values.
Downstream consumers can choose whether to trust materialized inferred facets or inspect the underlying judgments.
Schema work must model source tags, facets, judgments, and facet-materialization provenance distinctly.

title: Use a shared core schema with domain profiles
context: The project must support anime, TV, and movies without becoming anime-specific or forcing downstream consumers to build completely separate interfaces for each media type. Anime still needs first-class handling because its taxonomy includes concepts such as OVA, ONA, specials, cour/season structure, source demographics, and adaptation-line relationships that should not be flattened into generic TV fields.
decision: Use one shared core schema for cross-media consumption and add domain profile surfaces for domain-specific fields. Keep common concepts such as entities, titles, external IDs, relationships, evidence, provenance, facets, retrieval text, embeddings, judgments, and manifests in the shared interface. Put domain-specific facts in profiles such as `anime_profile`, `tv_profile`, and `movie_profile` when forcing them into the core would make the interface noisy or misleading.
consequences: Downstream implementers can build one primary interface across media types.
Anime remains first-class without making the whole dataset anime-shaped.
Schema implementation should separate cross-domain facets from domain-specific profile fields.

title: Allow partial enrichment with explicit enrichment status
context: The dataset needs to grow over time without requiring every entity to pass through every enrichment stage before publication. New or newly discovered works may first have only source IDs and titles, then later gain relationships, facets, judgments, retrieval text, embeddings, and derived similarity surfaces. Requiring complete enrichment before publication would slow updates and force unnecessary rebuilds.
decision: Allow v1 snapshots to publish partially enriched records as long as enrichment status is explicit at useful levels such as entity, table, recipe, and snapshot metadata. Treat enrichment stages as additive or recomputable where practical, so later snapshots can add or refresh evidence, facets, relationships, judgments, embeddings, and derived tables without re-ingesting sources or rebuilding unrelated base surfaces.
consequences: The dataset can grow incrementally while remaining useful to downstream consumers.
Consumers can decide whether to use records based on their enrichment status and available surfaces.
Implementation should track stage completion, pending work, recipe versions, and derived-surface recomputation boundaries.

title: Require meaningful anime, TV, and movie source paths for v1
context: The project scope is anime, TV, and movies. Anime is first-class and may be the richest early implementation spine, but treating anime as the implementation point would undermine the shared-core schema goal and make the project look anime-specific. V1 should prove the cross-domain artifact contract with real source paths for all three initial domains.
decision: Do not count an anime-only artifact as v1. V1 must include at least one meaningful anime source path, one meaningful TV source path, and one meaningful movie source path through the shared core schema, with domain profiles, source roles, provider-review evidence, publishable-field policy, provenance, titles, external IDs, and enough records to exercise the interface for each domain. Anime TV series, anime movies, incidental cross-reference IDs, runtime-only lookups, and empty profile tables do not satisfy the required TV or movie source paths.
consequences: Anime-heavy bootstrap work remains useful but is pre-v1 until TV and movie paths exist.
The v1 milestone validates the shared core plus domain profile architecture across all initial domains.
Implementation planning should prioritize a first TV and movie source path before declaring v1 complete.

title: Use a rich relationship taxonomy with grouping families
context: Relationship quality is a core value of the dataset. A small taxonomy would collapse important distinctions such as sequel versus prequel, adaptation versus alternate adaptation, remake versus reboot, special versus recap, and same-franchise versus shared-universe relationships. That would make the dataset less useful than existing fragmented sources.
decision: Use rich, precise relationship types where evidence supports them, and expose broader `relationship_family` values for grouping. Preserve direction for directional relationships through explicit types, inverse mappings, or paired edges where useful. Generic relationship families may be used for grouping and fallback, but they must not replace precise types when richer evidence is available.
consequences: Downstream consumers can build both simple grouped views and precise graph experiences from the same artifacts.
Relationship extraction and LLM judgment prompts should target precise labels instead of broad catch-all buckets.
Schema implementation must support sparse relationship types without forcing every source path to populate every type.

title: Treat vibes as consumer-side query interpretation
context: Users may ask for media with a certain feeling or vibe in normal prose, but those requests are inherently non-deterministic and depend on the consuming application's ranking and query strategy. The dataset should enable this class of search without pretending to own one universal interpretation or recommendation answer.
decision: Do not model `vibes` as a canonical deterministic dataset field. External consumers may use LLMs to interpret prose requests and then run RAG, vector search, graph traversal, filtering, or blended ranking over the dataset. This project should expose the information needed for those systems: facets, source tags where publishable, rich relationships, evidence, retrieval text, embeddings, judgments, materialized inferred facets where approved, provenance, and confidence metadata.
consequences: The dataset remains an artifact provider rather than a recommendation product.
Vibe-like discovery depends on making structured and retrieval-ready surfaces complete and explainable.
LLM interpretation of user prose, RAG prompts, ranking policy, and final presentation remain consumer responsibilities.

title: Treat human review as optional manual intervention
context: The dataset should be able to run on a schedule and progressively enhance itself. Human review can be useful for corrections or difficult cases, but requiring human approval would turn scheduled refresh and enhancement into a manual workflow and slow down new-source/new-record availability.
decision: Do not make human review a required pipeline gate. Scheduled ingest, normalization, relationship extraction, facet generation, judgment execution, retrieval text, embeddings, and derived enhancement jobs should be able to run without human intervention. When manual review or correction exists, capture it as explicit judgment or correction artifacts with provenance and recipe/status metadata.
consequences: The pipeline remains automatable and schedule-friendly.
Manual intervention remains available for corrections and high-value review without blocking normal enhancement.
Schema work should support human-origin judgments/corrections but not require them for publication.

title: Leave room for books as a future progressive domain
context: Books and source material matter for adaptations and media relationships, but v1 scope remains anime, TV, and movies. The project should not add books as a full browse domain yet, but it should avoid schema choices that make future book support difficult.
decision: Use books and source material as relationship evidence in v1, not as a full browse domain. Leave room for a future accepted decision to promote books into a progressive domain with its own meaningful source path, provider review, domain profile, artifact surfaces, and enhancement stages.
consequences: V1 can model source-material relationships without broadening the browse-domain scope immediately.
Future book support remains compatible with the shared core plus domain profile architecture.
Implementation should avoid hard-coding assumptions that only anime, TV, and movie can ever have domain profiles.

title: Publish Parquet tables plus a manifest as the consumption contract
context: The project is a dataset compiler, not an API or direct application. Downstream systems that consume the output should be able to read durable files and decide for themselves whether to load them into search indexes, graph stores, databases, notebooks, RAG pipelines, or applications. DuckDB can be useful to consumers, but making a DuckDB database artifact part of the project contract would imply a consumption layer that the project does not intend to own.
decision: Define the canonical published artifact as Parquet tables plus a manifest. Do not publish a hosted API, GraphQL endpoint, application-specific query service, or DuckDB database artifact as the consumption contract. Use the manifest to describe artifact paths, table names, schema versions, row counts, snapshot metadata, recipe versions, source coverage, and enrichment status.
consequences: The dataset remains portable and file-based for downstream implementers.
Consumers can load Parquet into DuckDB or any other system without this repo owning those consumption choices.
Implementation planning should prioritize stable Parquet schemas and manifest quality over API or database packaging work.

title: Use Hugging Face commits and tags for dataset snapshot identity
context: Hugging Face Dataset Hub is the preferred publication location because it is built for reusable datasets, supports large dataset files better than GitHub LFS for this use case, integrates with Parquet and dataset discovery, and fits the project boundary of publishing files rather than serving an API. Hugging Face dataset repos are Git-backed, so consumers can use branches, tags, or full commit SHAs as revisions.
decision: Publish Parquet artifacts and the manifest to a Hugging Face dataset repo. Treat each publish commit as a physical immutable snapshot, treat `main` as the moving latest pointer, use tags for supported release snapshots, and record the full Hugging Face commit SHA in the manifest. Do not duplicate every historical snapshot as dated folders on `main`; use repository history, tags, and commit SHAs for snapshot identity.
consequences: Consumers can choose convenience by following `main` or reproducibility by pinning a tag or full commit SHA.
The manifest becomes the bridge between dataset-level semantic versions, schema/source/recipe versions, and the physical Hugging Face revision.
Publication tooling should capture and validate the Hugging Face commit SHA after upload.

title: Use compatibility tiers for core, profile, derived, and experimental surfaces
context: The dataset will expose one shared core schema plus domain profiles and derived surfaces. Downstream developers need the shared core to remain stable across snapshots, while anime, TV, movie, future book, judgment, and derived enhancement surfaces need room to evolve as taxonomy and recipes improve.
decision: Treat the shared core schema as the stable public contract and keep it backward-compatible within a major dataset/schema version. Domain profiles and extended surfaces are independently versioned and may evolve faster, including breaking changes when those changes are explicitly versioned and declared in the manifest. Use compatibility tiers: `core` is stable, `profile` is independently versioned, `derived` is recipe-versioned and recomputable, and `experimental` has no compatibility guarantee but must be clearly marked.
consequences: Downstream consumers can rely on one cross-domain core loader across compatible snapshots.
Domain-specific taxonomy can improve without forcing every consumer to upgrade immediately.
Manifest quality becomes critical because it must declare schema versions, recipe versions, experimental status, and compatibility notices.

title: Keep entity IDs stable within a dataset line with explicit identity changes
context: Downstream consumers need stable entity IDs for joins, saved references, graph traversal, search indexes, and application state. At the same time, media identity is imperfect: published records may later need merges, splits, redirects, deprecations, withdrawals, or supersessions. The project also needs an escape hatch if the identity premise itself is found to be fundamentally broken.
decision: Treat `entity_id` values as durable and non-reusable within a dataset line. Model identity corrections through explicit identity-change surfaces for merges, splits, redirects, deprecations, withdrawals, and supersessions rather than silently changing IDs. If the identity model or dataset premise is fundamentally broken, the project may start a new dataset line, repository, or version family while leaving the old line published for existing consumers.
consequences: Consumers can store and join entity IDs confidently within a dataset line.
Identity corrections remain auditable and migratable across snapshots.
The project is not locked into preserving a known-broken premise forever; major premise failures can move to a corrected dataset line.

title: Keep provenance separate and expose confidence on core tables
context: From a practical consumer and classical database perspective, inline field-level provenance adds size and complexity without enough direct value for most downstream applications. Consumers usually need confidence, evidence strength, conflict status, and quality flags more than they need a full source lineage trail for every field.
decision: Model provenance as separate joinable artifact surfaces rather than inline field-level metadata by default. Core consumption tables should expose lightweight references and practical trust signals such as `confidence`, `evidence_count`, `conflict_status`, `quality_flags`, `evidence_id`, `provenance_id`, and `recipe_version`. Field-level provenance is not required by default and should be reserved for exceptional audit, debug, or conflict-resolution surfaces.
consequences: Core Parquet tables stay easier to consume and smaller.
Consumers can use confidence and evidence signals for product behavior without joining audit tables on every query.
The project remains auditable through separate provenance, evidence, source snapshot, provider run, and recipe run tables.
